response: Before the pandemic, remote work was uncommon, with fewer than 5% of workers fully remote. In 2020 it became a forced mainstream experiment enabled by tools like Zoom and Slack and backed by cloud infrastructure, though productivity studies (including Stanford’s Nick Bloom research) found mixed results. As companies pushed return-to-office policies, backlash grew and hybrid models became more common, alongside concerns about commercial real estate and “geographic arbitrage” as workers moved to lower-cost areas. Remote work also reshaped company culture and slowed or changed junior employee development, while “digital nomad” visas emerged to formalize cross-border remote work.
